text: Best of the Last 6 Months — H1 2026

Six months of our beats, distilled into one keepsake issue. Below is the half-year's standout reading and tools across AI engineering, free & open AI, networking, applied AI how-to, and Android — plus the foundation set for our newest beat, ready-to-use AI assets. Nothing here is this week's news; it's the pieces worth coming back to. Each section is also live on the archive site.

A pick of the half-year's standout reading in AI engineering — the pieces worth coming back to, drawn from across the first half of 2026 rather than any single week. One throughline ran through almost all of it: the model mattered less than everything wrapped around it. The sharpest writing sat a layer below the weights — in the harness and scaffolding, in how the KV cache is compressed and where it lives, in whether you can trust a benchmark or an ingested document at all.

title: The harness is the product — where agent quality actually lives
desc: A clear synthesis of how production AI systems win: not the model, but the loop, context, tools, safety and evals around it, with a usable taxonomy for reasoning about each layer.

title: Fifteen models, one afternoon, big coding gains — by changing only the harness
desc: A rigorous ablation showing the edit-tool format an agent uses is a dominant lever on coding success. A content-hash "hashline" format that lets the model reference stable line IDs beats patch and str_replace; one model jumped roughly tenfold on the format change alone.

title: A reliability layer for self-hosted tool-calling
desc: Forge is a guardrail/middleware layer that drops onto an existing agent loop and sharply lifts tool-calling accuracy — an 8B local model from single digits to the mid-80s, and a frontier model from 85% to 98% — with a published eval harness behind the numbers.

title: You fixed the rate limits — now your agent fails quietly
desc: A useful read on how the usual reliability moves — retries, fallbacks, caches — buy uptime at the expense of correctness, and how to separate the two with idempotency keys and trust-tagging across a chain of steps.

title: A maintained catalog of agentic-coding patterns
desc: Simon Willison's opinionated, annotated reference — subagents, red/green TDD, real prompts and reusable templates — built to be returned to rather than read once.

title: Quantization from the ground up
desc: An end-to-end explainer with interactive visualizations, working code, and measured quality/speed tradeoffs — symmetric and asymmetric quantization grounded in real perplexity, KL-divergence and speed numbers with reproducible commands.

title: Losslessly compressing the KV cache around 4x
desc: A cheap predictor model plus an arithmetic coder compresses the KV cache losslessly by roughly 4x in bf16, and about 8x combined with FP8. The mechanism is fully spelled out: both sides reconstruct deterministically from the same prompt.

title: The KV cache is becoming the memory hierarchy of inference
desc: A clarifying framing that treats the cache as a multi-tier hierarchy — GPU memory, host, remote storage — that serving stacks now manage the way CPUs manage caches, tying prefix caching, eviction and cross-tier offload into one picture.

title: How a vLLM-style inference engine works
desc: A walkthrough of a roughly 1,200-line serving engine — scheduler queues, continuous batching, block-based KV cache and prefix caching, tensor parallelism — at a level you could re-implement without a GPU cluster.

title: We replaced RAG with a virtual filesystem for our docs assistant
desc: Instead of chunk retrieval, a virtual filesystem intercepts grep/cat/ls/find and translates them into vector queries, letting the agent explore docs like a codebase. Backed by production metrics at scale, including a P90 cold-start drop from tens of seconds to about 100ms.

title: How to index images for RAG
desc: Caption images once at indexing time with a cheap vision model that sees the surrounding document context, then store the captions as text chunks — measurably better answers than query-time multimodal, with the cost and latency tradeoffs reported across real projects.

title: Scoring ~100% on eight agent benchmarks without solving a single task
desc: An automated agent gamed eight major leaderboards — Terminal-Bench, SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, OSWorld and more — via pytest hooks, config reads, weak validators and judge prompt-injection. Essential reading before trusting any agent benchmark or building an eval harness.

title: How attackers poison the documents your RAG system trusts
desc: Injecting three fabricated documents into a knowledge base made the model confidently report false figures — a 95% attack success rate with no jailbreak — paired with a defense table showing how much each ingestion-time guard actually buys you.

text: A look back at the genuinely free and open finds of the past six months — not this week's news, but the offers and tools from across the first half of 2026 that are still worth grabbing. It was a strong stretch for open weights, with frontier-class families like DeepSeek V4 and GLM-5 shipping under permissive licences you can actually download and run. Below are the picks that hold up: open models you own, free inference tiers you can claim today, and self-hostable tools with no trial clock.

title: DeepSeek V4 lands with open weights and a million-token context
desc: A frontier-class mixture-of-experts family (V4-Pro and the lighter V4-Flash) released MIT-licensed on Hugging Face with a 1M-token context — free to download, self-host, and use commercially. Flash is realistically self-hostable; Pro is servable cheaply.

title: GLM-5 — a 744B open-weight model under MIT
desc: Zhipu's 744B-parameter (40B active) mixture-of-experts, released under the MIT license on Hugging Face, with cheap hosted access via Z.ai and OpenRouter if you lack the GPUs. One of the largest fully-MIT open-weight models to date.

title: Kimi K2.6 — a 1T-parameter open-weights coding model
desc: Moonshot AI's 1T-param mixture-of-experts (32B active, 256K context, natively multimodal and agentic) under a Modified MIT License — code and weights both free to download. A strong open alternative to the backends behind paid coding agents.

title: Qwen3.6 — the Qwen sizes you can actually self-host
desc: Alibaba's Qwen3.6-27B and 35B-A3B shipped under Apache 2.0 on Hugging Face and ModelScope — free to download, fine-tune, and run on prosumer GPUs. The flagship Qwen3.6-Max is closed; these mid-size tiers are the free path.

title: Cerebras is giving away a million tokens a day, no card required
desc: The free developer tier grants a reported 1,000,000 tokens/day across hosted models (Llama Scout, Qwen3, gpt-oss-120B) at very high throughput, no credit card and no waitlist — enough to run real workloads at zero cost, with the usual per-minute rate caps.

title: Mistral's free "Experiment" plan — just a phone number
desc: Free API access to Mistral's open and proprietary models (including Large and Codestral) with no credit card, just a verified phone number. Worth heeding the plan's own caveat: Experiment requests may be used to train Mistral's models, so keep sensitive data out.

title: OpenRouter — one key, dozens of free models
desc: The free tier exposes a couple of dozen :free models (DeepSeek R1, Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen3 Coder, Gemini Flash and more) with no credit card. A one-time, non-expiring $10 top-up permanently raises the daily request cap — a cheap, simple way to try many open models before committing.

title: Groq — fast free inference on email sign-up
desc: Instant API access to fast open models like Llama 3.3 70B with published per-model free limits, no credit card — just email or Google sign-up. Reliable enough for light production and a good match for agents and chatbots.

title: OpenClaw — a self-hosted personal assistant you actually own
desc: The open-source breakout of the half-year: an MIT-licensed personal AI agent that runs on your own devices, connecting LLMs to 50+ integrations with data that never leaves your machine. Free to self-host; bring your own model keys.

title: Dify — a free, self-hostable platform for AI apps and agents
desc: An Apache 2.0 platform for building agentic workflows — visual builder, retrieval, model routing — free to self-host as a zero-cost alternative to paid LLM-app platforms. Bring your own model keys and run the whole stack yourself.

title: OpenHands — a free autonomous coding agent you can run locally
desc: An MIT-licensed software-engineering agent that edits code, runs commands, browses and calls APIs — free to self-host and point at local models via Ollama. Pair it with one of the open-weight models above for a zero-cost agentic-coding stack.

text: A look back at the networking writing worth keeping from the first half of 2026 — the pieces our beat kept returning to across service-provider routing, data-centre fabric, and the automation and telemetry that hold it all together. Nothing here is breaking news; these are the explainers, postmortems and design arguments that earned a second read.

title: Cloudflare: enforce the first AS in BGP paths to shut a hijack class
desc: A concrete, deployable control — verifying a peer always lists its own ASN first in the AS_PATH — and a clear account of the forged-path misdirection it prevents. Drop-in for any operator.

title: Catching short-lived BGP route leaks automatically
desc: Doug Madory on the ephemeral leaks that slip past manual review, and how to detect them without a human in the loop — measurement-backed routing-security automation.

title: RADIUS isn't going away, so let's fix it properly
desc: The FreeRADIUS maintainer makes the case for modernising a load-bearing piece of legacy AAA, and lays out the security roadmap from the primary source.

title: Stop designing route reflectors like it's 2005
desc: Opinionated, current guidance on iBGP route-reflector placement and scaling, challenging design habits that have outlived their justification.

title: Goodbye, leaf-and-spine networks?
desc: Ivan Pepelnjak weighs AWS's random-graph-theory fabric claims against the established Clos default — a sharp, skeptical look at whether the new design actually replaces the old one.

title: EVPN centralized routing with Arista EOS
desc: A lab-validated deep-dive into EVPN route-generation patterns for centralized routing, with mechanics that carry across vendors.

title: How Discord moved voice to the edge
desc: A first-party account of re-architecting real-time UDP voice onto edge points-of-presence with anycast and steering — a large-scale deployment told with its trade-offs intact.

title: Scaling Akvorado's routing store with sharding
desc: Vincent Bernat shards the BMP-fed routing store to push flow-enrichment past a single-store bottleneck — a reusable pattern for anyone building telemetry pipelines, straight from the tool's author.

title: Understanding traceroute by reimplementing it in Rust
desc: George Michaelson teaches the internals — and how to read traceroute output correctly — by rebuilding it from scratch. Diagnostic literacy you'll reuse constantly.

title: Managing MikroTik RouterOS in natural language with MikroMCP
desc: A working MCP server that exposes a real network OS to AI-native control — one of the more concrete uses of LLMs for network operations to surface this half, rather than another think-piece.

text: Six months of applied-AI reading, distilled. We pulled the half-year's most useful how-to material on prompt and context engineering, retrieval, evals, and Claude power-use — the pieces that explain why a technique works, not just that it does. These aren't this week's links; they're the references worth keeping open in a tab.

title: Finding the smallest set of high-signal tokens
desc: Anthropic's canonical framing for what to put into — and deliberately keep out of — an agent's context, with practical patterns you can apply immediately.

title: Context editing and the memory tool, with numbers
desc: The two primitives that keep long agent runs inside budget: auto-clearing stale tool results near the limit, and a file-based memory tool. Worth reading for the measured gains — context editing alone lifted agentic task performance by roughly 29%, and with memory by roughly 39%, while cutting token use sharply on a 100-turn eval.

title: Harnesses for long-running agents
desc: A two-agent scaffold — an initializer plus an incremental coding agent — backed by progress files, structured feature lists and git checkpoints, so an agent keeps making real progress across many context windows instead of losing the thread or declaring "done" early.

title: Context as infrastructure you build
desc: Eugene Yan's mental model for compounding leverage with AI — context as infrastructure, taste as configuration, verification for autonomy, delegation for scale. A clean reframe of context as something you engineer rather than text you paste.

title: Claude prompting best practices
desc: Anthropic's continuously-updated reference covering clarity, few-shot, XML structuring, long-context placement, thinking, and tool use — each technique paired with a rationale and a before/after example. The one prompt reference worth bookmarking.

title: Writing agent system prompts, learned from reverse-engineering Claude Code
desc: Treat a system prompt as environment design: identity and safety at the top and bottom, principles over rigid step lists, static and dynamic content kept separate, and always explaining the why so the model generalizes to edge cases. Unusually all-rationale.

title: Latent-space engineering: priming the model's frame of mind
desc: Jesse Vincent's argument that prompts work better when they put the model in an optimal state — calm, confident, stylistically primed — and a mechanistic reason why supportive role-framing changes output quality, beyond "be nice to the AI."

title: Contextual retrieval, with a runnable eval harness
desc: Prepend a Claude-generated sentence of context to each chunk before indexing, then layer hybrid search and reranking. The cookbook shows the measured payoff — retrieval failures dropping by roughly half end-to-end — and states the ingestion-cost trade-off honestly.

title: The LLM evals FAQ everyone keeps sharing
desc: Hamel Husain's no-nonsense answers to the questions teams actually have: build application-specific judges from real error analysis, validate them before you trust them, and skip the generic metrics that create false confidence. The practitioner reference on the topic.

title: A pragmatic guide to LLM evals for developers
desc: Where and how to run evals without overbuilding — at three points: offline, in production, and pre-merge — kept cheap and meaningful.

title: Eight practical tips for writing agent skills
desc: Philipp Schmid's concrete, field-tested advice for getting more out of skills — the small authoring decisions that separate a skill the model actually uses from one it ignores.

title: A minimal, production Claude Code setup
desc: One scoped CLAUDE.md, an MCP config with only the servers you actually use, a single safety hook, one reusable skill, subagents only where they earn their keep. Every component is justified by a trade-off — the opposite of a feature dump — and backed by four months of daily use.

title: The comprehensive guide to building agents
desc: Chip Huyen's deep, vendor-neutral overview of how agents actually work — still the best single starting point if you're building one.

text: Six months of Android, distilled to what actually earned a place on a phone. Two threads ran through the half: a genuine fight over who gets to install what — Google's developer-verification push and the F-Droid-led pushback on sideloading — and a quiet, steady stream of local-first tools that keep your data on the device instead of in someone's cloud. Below are the standouts, weighted toward privacy, open source, and things that do one job well.

title: A local-first terminal companion (Conduit)
desc: A clean SSH, Mosh and SFTP client that keeps your sessions and keys on the device rather than in someone's cloud — a solid pick if you manage servers from your phone.

title: A database editor for your pocket (Basepanel)
desc: Poke at Postgres, MySQL and SQLite databases straight from your phone, no laptop required. Worth checking how it handles credentials before you wire it to anything sensitive.

title: Test on a phone you don't own (Tapflow)
desc: Self-hosted streaming of a running mobile simulator to your device — handy for QA across screen sizes you don't have hardware for.

title: ADB without a computer (80bee)
desc: Brings the Android debugging toolkit directly onto the device, so tinkerers can run developer commands without plugging into a PC.

title: Build Android apps from any coding agent
desc: Google's own tooling for driving Android builds from any coding agent — the platform meeting the agentic-development moment.

title: Expense tracking that never leaves your phone (PennyWise)
desc: Turns your bank's transaction texts into a tidy, searchable spending timeline, with categorisation running entirely on-device — no cloud, no sign-up. The honest caveat: it needs broad access to your messages to read those texts in the first place.

title: Log and intelligently block notifications (DoNotNotify)
desc: A utility that captures and selectively silences notifications — the rare app that cuts noise instead of adding to it.

title: A Chromium fork with real extension support (ElixirBrowser)
desc: A community browser bringing desktop-style extension support to Android, for people who want a fuller browsing setup on a phone.

title: Manage Telegram proxies (MTProto Hub)
desc: A clean single-purpose tool for collecting, testing and using MTProto proxies — niche, but exactly what you want if you depend on them for connectivity.

title: Keep Android Open — the case against locking down sideloading
desc: F-Droid's rallying post as Google's developer-verification plans came into focus — the clearest statement of what's at stake for sideloading and independent app distribution.

title: Google's 24-hour process for sideloading unverified apps
desc: The concrete policy at the centre of that debate, laid out plainly — the rules everyone is actually arguing about.

text: This beat is only a couple of weeks old, so think of this less as a six-month retrospective and more as the foundation set — the ready-to-use AI assets to start from if you're building a working toolkit for Claude, ChatGPT and Gemini today. A few are long-standing staples that have earned their place; a few genuinely shipped this fortnight. Here are the strongest, weighted toward the prompts and skills first.

title: A skill that rewrites your prompt for you (Claude)
desc: An Agent Skill that quietly refines whatever you type for the model currently running — a rare skill that is itself about better prompting.

title: Make your agent think like a blunt senior dev (Claude)
desc: Gives an agent the instincts of a no-nonsense senior engineer. It went viral this fortnight — thousands of stars in days — which says something about the appetite for opinionated defaults.

title: Pressure-test a product idea before you build it (Claude)
desc: A skill from a long-time product manager that interrogates a beginner's product idea and surfaces the holes early, before you've written any code.

title: Patterns and starters for agent loops (Claude)
desc: Practical patterns, starter templates and CLI tools for "loop engineering" an agent, from prolific LLM practitioner Cobus Greyling.

title: A library of ready-to-run prompt patterns (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini)
desc: An open-source collection of curated prompts for everyday tasks — summarise, extract, analyse, write — that you can run from the command line or lift into any assistant. The single best starting point for reusable prompts.

title: The system prompts behind the major AI tools (any assistant)
desc: The actual system prompts of Claude Code, Cursor and the rest, collected in one place. Less a prompt to copy than a masterclass in how the best tools instruct a model — well worth borrowing from.

title: A browsable library of reusable agent "rules" (coding agents)
desc: A community catalogue of rule files — system-prompt-style instruction sets — for coding agents, sorted by language and framework. A faster way to pick up a battle-tested setup than writing one from scratch.

title: Keep your assistant's docs current (Claude, Cursor — MCP)
desc: An MCP server that injects up-to-date library documentation into your editor, so the model stops writing against APIs that changed months ago.

title: Give a coding agent real eyes on the page (coding agents — MCP)
desc: The official server that lets an agent inspect and debug live web pages through Chrome DevTools — invaluable for front-end work.

title: Path-scoped rules and skills for a whole team (coding agents — MCP)
desc: An MCP server that gives a team shared, path-scoped memories, rules and skills, so the agent picks up the right conventions for whichever part of the repo it's working in.

title: The running catalogue of MCP servers
desc: The community's go-to index of Model Context Protocol servers, sorted by category — the fastest way to find a server for a given tool or data source.

title: A large, browsable prompt collection (any assistant)
desc: Formerly "Awesome ChatGPT Prompts": a huge, searchable community library you can copy from, across assistants and use-cases.
